planting.note: Plant plugs or sod in late spring once soil reaches 60°F; direct-seeded beds benefit from pre-chilled (stratified) seed and consistent moisture until established.

directions: Plant plugs or sod pieces 12–18 inches apart in full sun and well-drained soil after the last frost, pressing firmly to ensure good soil contact. Water weekly for the first season while roots establish, then reduce to deep, infrequent irrigation once the grass knits together. Mow once or twice a year at 3–4 inches to remove thatch and encourage fresh growth, or leave unmowed for a true prairie-lawn effect. The grass will go straw-brown during winter dormancy and revive to blue-green in late spring.

planting.note: Sow seed shallowly on prepared, firm seedbed once soil temperatures consistently exceed 65°F; stolon-grown transplants establish considerably faster than seed.

directions: Broadcast seed lightly over a firm, weed-suppressed seedbed in late spring, pressing seed into the soil surface rather than covering deeply, as germination requires light and warmth. Keep the seedbed moist for three to four weeks until seedlings are visible, then transition to infrequent deep watering to encourage deep rooting. Once established, this grass spreads by stolons to fill gaps and form a cohesive turf that tolerates heavy foot traffic and prolonged drought. Avoid excess nitrogen fertilization, which promotes weedy competition over the grass itself.

planting.note: Sow into heavy clay or clay-loam soils in late spring; tolerates seasonally wet planting sites and performs best in low spots where water collects briefly.

directions: Direct sow into clay or clay-loam soils after the last frost when daytime temperatures are reliably warm, pressing seed into the surface and keeping moist until germination. Tobosa is naturally adapted to sites that flood seasonally and then bake dry, so it thrives in low spots, swales, or compacted areas where few other plants succeed. Space clumps 18–24 inches apart and avoid adding organic amendments to the planting hole, as this grass prefers lean, alkaline conditions. Once established, clumps require no supplemental water and should be left uncut to provide year-round ground cover and nesting habitat for quail and sparrows.

planting.note: Sow seed in fall to allow cool, moist winter conditions to break dormancy naturally; fall planting mimics the grass's native germination cycle and produces stronger first-year plants.

directions: Sow seed in fall on rocky or fast-draining soil in partial shade, particularly under native oaks or chaparral shrubs where summer soil moisture is minimal. Scratch seed lightly into the surface and allow winter rains to do most of the watering; supplemental irrigation in the first spring helps seedlings size up before summer dormancy. Plants go partially or fully dormant in summer heat, so site them where the dried foliage will be screened by surrounding plants. Once established, clumps self-sow modestly and can be divided in early fall to expand a planting.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring into rocky or sandy, well-drained upland soil; can also be fall-seeded to allow cold stratification over winter for stronger spring germination.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in spring on exposed, well-drained soil after the last frost, or fall-sow to let winter cold naturally stratify the seed for improved spring germination. Thin seedlings to 18–24 inches and avoid amending soil with compost, as plains muhly is native to lean, alkaline uplands and tends to flop in overly rich conditions. Plants form tidy, wiry clumps that produce airy seed panicles from late summer into fall, providing both visual interest and forage for native sparrows and finches. Cut clumps back to 4–6 inches in late winter before new growth resumes to keep the planting looking fresh.
